sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: Ahead is the Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park, where Sweet Auburn opens as neighborhood ground and national freedom terrain at the same time.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at the Martin Luther King, Jr. district, a powerful opening point for reading Auburn Avenue through both local life and national civil-rights memory.

sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: Next is Ebenezer Baptist Church, where Black church life, ministry, and movement leadership converge inside one of Atlanta's defining sacred spaces.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at Ebenezer Baptist Church, where the route turns fully into Black church life, public voice, and the leadership traditions that shaped Sweet Auburn.

sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: Approaching The King Center, the route moves from neighborhood history into the institutional care of movement memory and public ritual.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at The King Center, where the story of Sweet Auburn expands into how Atlanta preserves, interprets, and extends the civil-rights legacy.

sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: Ahead is Auburn Avenue Research Library, where archives and stewardship help preserve the intellectual life of Black Atlanta.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at the Auburn Avenue Research Library, a crucial stop for thinking about how Black Atlanta remembers itself through collections, scholarship, and public history.

sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: Next is Big Bethel A.M.E. Church, which widens the route beyond one family or one movement story into the broader church infrastructure of Auburn Avenue.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at Big Bethel A.M.E. Church, a strong place to read Sweet Auburn through Black worship, civic life, and institutional continuity.

sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.drive: The route closes at Prince Hall Masonic Temple, where Black enterprise, fraternal power, and media history help complete the Auburn Avenue picture.
sweet-auburn-black-enterprise-and-civil-.walk: You are at the Prince Hall site, a fitting capstone for a route about Black enterprise, civic infrastructure, and the wider institutional world of Sweet Auburn.

atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.drive: Ahead is Woodruff Library, the shared intellectual commons of the AUC and a strong opening point for Atlanta's Black scholarly ecosystem.
atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.walk: You are at Woodruff Library, where the route begins with archives, research, and the shared infrastructure that links Atlanta's major Black colleges.

atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.drive: Next is Clark Atlanta University, where the route moves into the scale and civic reach of Black higher education in Atlanta.
atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.walk: You are at Clark Atlanta University, a key stop for reading the AUC as a living network of scholarship, leadership formation, and public service.

atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.drive: Approaching Morehouse College, the route enters one of the city's most influential sites for Black leadership, ministry, and intellectual formation.
atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.walk: You are at Morehouse College, an indispensable anchor in Atlanta's story of Black intellectual capital and public voice.

atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.drive: Next is Spelman College, where Black women's intellectual leadership becomes central to the route rather than secondary to it.
atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.walk: You are at Spelman College, a necessary stop for understanding Atlanta's educational legacy through Black women's scholarship, leadership, and institutional power.

atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.drive: The route closes at Morehouse School of Medicine, where scholarship turns directly toward care, medicine, and Black professional service.
atlanta-university-center-and-black-inte.walk: You are at Morehouse School of Medicine, a fitting capstone that links Black intellectual life to health, public service, and institutional ambition.

civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.drive: Ahead is the National Center for Civil and Human Rights, where Atlanta's civil-rights legacy is framed inside a broader public history of freedom and human dignity.
civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.walk: You are at the Center for Civil and Human Rights, a strong starting point for reading Atlanta's movement legacy through a public-facing national lens.

civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.drive: Next is Atlanta City Hall, where the route turns from movement memory toward the city-scale institutions that exercise public power.
civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.walk: You are at Atlanta City Hall, a useful place to think about how local governance, executive leadership, and civic decision-making shape the city.

civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.drive: Approaching the Georgia State Capitol, the route adds state power, law, and protest geography to Atlanta's story of civil rights.
civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.walk: You are at the Georgia State Capitol, where public memory meets law, policy, and the wider political machinery of the state.

civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.drive: Ahead is The King Center, where the route returns to the stewardship of movement memory and the public ritual of remembrance.
civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.walk: You are at The King Center, where Atlanta's role in civil-rights memory is held, interpreted, and carried forward into the present.

civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.drive: The route closes at the Martin Luther King, Jr. district, where national civil-rights memory resolves back into neighborhood ground.
civil-rights-public-power-and-national-m.walk: You are at the Martin Luther King, Jr. National Historical Park, a fitting capstone for a route that moves between public power and the local places that gave it meaning.

black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.drive: Ahead is APEX Museum, where the route opens by tying Black Atlanta's contemporary culture back to the long civic and social life of Sweet Auburn.
black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.walk: You are at APEX Museum, a strong opening point for reading Black Atlanta's present creativity through deeper neighborhood and historical memory.

black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.drive: Next is the Trap Music Museum, where Atlanta's modern sound is presented as public history rather than just nightlife or celebrity culture.
black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.walk: You are at the Trap Music Museum, a clear anchor for Atlanta's contemporary music identity and the route's strongest modern-cultural signal.

black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.drive: Approaching the Black Music and Entertainment Walk of Fame district, the route turns toward how Atlanta publicly honors Black artists inside its modern cultural brand.
black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.walk: You are in the Walk of Fame district, a useful stop for thinking about how Atlanta celebrates Black music legacy in public space rather than only inside museums.

black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.drive: Next is Hammonds House Museum, where Black visual art, curatorial care, and West End cultural life bring another layer of Atlanta's creative identity into view.
black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.walk: You are at Hammonds House Museum, one of the city's strongest anchors for Black visual art and a key bridge between neighborhood history and contemporary culture.

black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.drive: The route closes on the BeltLine Westside Trail, where public art, redevelopment, and city movement make contemporary Atlanta legible in motion.
black-atlanta-arts-music-and-contemporar.walk: You are on the BeltLine Westside Trail, a fitting capstone for seeing how culture, circulation, and redevelopment shape the city around you.

west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.drive: Ahead is West End, where the route opens by treating the neighborhood itself as a historic record of transportation, preservation, and change.
west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.walk: You are in the West End Historic District, a strong opening point for reading neighborhood memory through buildings, streets, and the long history of urban change.

west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.drive: Next is Hammonds House Museum, where neighborhood memory and Black cultural life come into focus through visual art and institutional care.
west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.walk: You are at Hammonds House Museum, a key anchor for understanding West End not just as preserved architecture but as living Black cultural ground.

west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.drive: Approaching The Wren's Nest, the route adds another layer of West End's historic texture and reminds us that neighborhoods carry many overlapping narratives.
west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.walk: You are at The Wren's Nest, a landmark that helps the route hold literary memory and neighborhood depth alongside Black cultural and civic history.

west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.drive: Next is the Shrine of the Black Madonna, where West End's neighborhood life connects to Black spiritual, cultural, and political tradition.
west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.walk: You are at the Shrine of the Black Madonna, a meaningful stop for reading West End through Black institution-building, spirituality, and community identity.

west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.drive: The route closes on the BeltLine Southwest Trail, where preservation, public space, redevelopment, and neighborhood memory all press against one another much closer to West End itself.
west-end-beltline-and-neighborhood-chang.walk: You are on the BeltLine Southwest Trail, a fitting capstone for thinking about what changes, what survives, and who gets to shape the future of West End.
